text:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Start a Spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("JobPostingsAnalysis") \
    .getOrCreate()

# Suppress verbose Spark logs
spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("ERROR")

print("Spark session started successfully.")
print(f"Spark version: {spark.version}")

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
text:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Start a Spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("JobPostingsAnalysis") \
    .getOrCreate()

# Suppress verbose Spark logs
spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("ERROR")

print("Spark session started successfully.")
print(f"Spark version: {spark.version}")

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
text:
Load and Prepare the Dataset

# Load the CSV file into a Spark DataFrame
# Update this path if your CSV is located elsewhere
df = spark.read \
    .option("header", "true") \
    .option("inferSchema", "true") \
    .option("multiLine", "true") \
    .option("escape", "\"") \
    .csv("/home/ubuntu/a02-sum26-frjulie8-proj/data/lightcast_job_postings.csv")

# Register the DataFrame as a temporary SQL table
df.createOrReplaceTempView("jobs")

print("Dataset loaded and registered as SQL table 'jobs'.")

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
text:
Verify the Data

# Display the first five rows
print("First 5 rows:")
df.show(5, truncate=True)


# Show the schema (column names & data types)
print("Schema:")
df.printSchema()

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 1: How many job postings are in the dataset?
text:
Query 1: How many job postings are in the dataset?

result_q1 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_job_postings
    FROM jobs
""")

result_q1.show()

Insight: This query counts the total number of rows in the dataset, giving us the overall size of the Lightcast job postings collection. Each row represents one job posting.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 2: Find the top 5 most common job titles
text:
Query 2: Find the top 5 most common job titles

result_q2 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT TITLE_NAME, COUNT(*) AS posting_count
    FROM jobs
    GROUP BY TITLE_NAME
    ORDER BY posting_count DESC
    LIMIT 5
""")

result_q2.show(truncate=False)

Insight: This query identifies the most in-demand roles in the dataset. The top job titles reveal which positions employers are hiring for most frequently, offering a snapshot of labor market demand.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 3: Find the average salary for each employment type
text:
Query 3: Find the average salary for each employment type

result_q3 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME,
        ROUND(AVG(SALARY), 2) AS avg_salary,
        COUNT(*) AS job_count
    FROM jobs
    WHERE SALARY IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY EMPLOYMENT_TYPE_NAME
    ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
""")

result_q3.show(truncate=False)

Insight: Comparing average salaries across employment types (e.g., Full-time vs. Part-time vs. Contract) highlights compensation differences. Full-time roles typically command higher average salaries than part-time or contract positions.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 4: What five states have the most job postings?
text:
Query 4: What five states have the most job postings?

result_q4 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT STATE_NAME, COUNT(*) AS posting_count
    FROM jobs
    WHERE STATE_NAME IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY STATE_NAME
    ORDER BY posting_count DESC
    LIMIT 5
""")

result_q4.show(truncate=False)

Insight: This query surfaces the states with the highest concentration of job postings, indicating regional hiring activity. States like California, Texas, and New York typically dominate job posting volumes due to their large economies.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 5: Salary range (max − min) for each job title in California
text:
Query 5: Salary range (max − min) for each job title in California

result_q5 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        TITLE_NAME,
        MAX(SALARY) AS max_salary,
        MIN(SALARY) AS min_salary,
        ROUND(MAX(SALARY) - MIN(SALARY), 2) AS salary_range
    FROM jobs
    WHERE STATE_NAME = 'California'
      AND SALARY IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY TITLE_NAME
    ORDER BY salary_range DESC
    LIMIT 10
""")

result_q5.show(truncate=False)

Insight: A wide salary range for a job title in California suggests significant variation across employers or experience levels. Roles with large ranges (like Software Engineer or Data Scientist) often have tiered compensation structures.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
section: Query 6: Top 5 industries with highest average salaries and more than 100 job postings
text:
Query 6: Top 5 industries with highest average salaries and more than 100 job postings

result_q6 = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        NAICS2_NAME AS industry,
        ROUND(AVG(SALARY), 2) AS avg_salary,
        COUNT(*) AS job_count
    FROM jobs
    WHERE SALARY IS NOT NULL
      AND NAICS2_NAME IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY NAICS2_NAME
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 100
    ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
    LIMIT 5
""")

result_q6.show(truncate=False)

Insight: This query filters to industries with meaningful sample sizes (>100 postings) and ranks them by average salary. High-paying industries with many postings — such as Finance or Technology — represent the most lucrative and active sectors in the labor market.

title: Spark SQL Job Data Analysis
text:
Close Spark Session

spark.stop()
print("Spark session stopped.")
